title: House Passes Lee Resolution Supporting National Black AIDS Awareness Day
text:
02.04.07
			 House Passes Lee Resolution Supporting National Black AIDS Awareness Day 
			(Washington, DC)  Today, by a vote of 396-0, the House of Representatives approved a resolution introduced by Congresswoman Barbara Lee (D-Oakland) to recognize and support the goals and ideals of National Black HIV/AIDS Awareness Day, which is this Wednesday, February 7th. Twenty-five years after the first cases of AIDS were reported, HIV is still running rampant in the United States, and it is African Americans who are bearing the brunt of this resurging epidemic, said Lee.  Unfortunately, today to be black is to be at greater risk of HIV/AIDS.  As Members of Congress we have a responsibility to change that.  I urge my colleagues to join with me in supporting National Black HIV/AIDS Awareness Day and making stopping the spread of this global pandemic a priority at home and abroad. Lee's resolution, H. Con. Res. 35, is designed to recognize and support the goals and ideals of National Black HIV/AIDS Awareness Day and encourage state and local governments, public health agencies and the media to emphasize and publicize the importance of this day among the African American community, and all communities. According to the Centers for Disease Control, African Americans accounted for almost half of all new HIV infections in 2005. All told, African American's represent 42 percent of all people currently living with HIV/AIDS, despite only representing 12.3 percent of the population (according to the 2000 Census).  Even worse, are the statistics for African American women, who accounted for 66 percent of all new HIV/AIDS cases among women in 2005, and were 25 times more likely to be infected than white women. National Black HIV/AIDS Awareness Day was created in 2001 by a coalition of five national non-profit organizations to raise awareness about HIV/AIDS among the African American community.  Recognized each year on February 7th, National Black HIV/AIDS Awareness Day encourages African Americans and all Americans to Get Educated, Get Involved, and Get Tested. ### 
	
	  

	
	
	
	
	
	
	

 
	    	  
		    
		    	Next Article

title: Barbara Lee Speaks Out Against Escalation  Calls for Fully Funding Withdrawal
text:
02.13.07
			 Barbara Lee Speaks Out Against Escalation  Calls for Fully Funding Withdrawal  
			(Washington, DC)    Today on the floor of the House of Representatives, Congresswoman Barbara Lee (D-Oakland) spoke forcefully in support of a bipartisan resolution supporting U.S. troops and rejecting President Bush's proposed escalation of the war in Iraq.  The following is her statement (as delivered): As the daughter of a proud veteran of two wars, I know personally that we have a moral obligation to support and protect our brave men and women on the ground in Iraq. However, there is no reason for us to stand behind the President's plan to escalate his failed policy in Iraq. In fact, Madame Speaker, the American people are ahead of us.  A USA Today/Gallup poll released just today shows that 60 percent oppose the escalation.  Sixty-three percent favor bringing our troops home by the end of 2008. And Last November the American people soundly rejected the President's failed policy on Iraq at the voting booth. You would think that the President understood what this all meant. After the election he continued his listening tour on options for Iraq.  But it seems that he wasn't hearing what the American people were saying. The Iraq Study Group actually indicated and said very clearly there was no military solution to this mess.  Rather than heed the call of military experts, advisors, the ISG, and the American people, the President offered an even worse plan: put more troops in harm's way in Iraq. This just doesn't make any sense.  That's why this no-confidence resolution puts the administration on notice: end the occupation and bring our troops home.   However, if the President doesn't change course we must go further. The war has undermined our credibility and standing in the world.  This war has cost too many lives and injured too many of our troops.  This war has cost too many Iraqi lives.  This war has cost us nearly half a trillion dollars and the costs keep mounting.  The chaos in Iraq that the President set in motion has further destabilized an already precarious balance in the Middle East.  We must take steps to use the upcoming supplemental appropriations bill to set in motion an end to this terrible and misguided war and bring our troops home from Iraq.  To that end, I support fully funding the safe withdrawal of our troops from Iraq over a six month period and I will work with my colleagues to do this. Additionally, along with my colleagues in the Progressive Caucus and the Out of Iraq Caucus, we have introduced H.R. 508, the Bring Our Troops Home and Sovereignty of Iraq Restoration Act.  This bill would completely fully fund military withdrawal from Iraq within six months, while ensuring that our troops and contractors leave safely, and accelerate the training of Iraqi security forces.  And we would make certain that our veterans, who have given so much, receive the health and mental health benefits that they deserve. Our bill would remove the specter of an endless and that's what this is right now, it's an endless occupation   by preventing the establishment of permanent military bases.  Our very presence in Iraq is fueling the insurgency and our troops have been targets of this civil war. Madame Speaker, these are the best and safest ways to end this occupation. But it really didn't have to be this way.  Imagine for a moment what would have happened had Congress adopted my substitute amendment to the authorization to use force against Iraq in October 2002?  We would have allowed the UN inspectors to finish their jobs. We would have discovered what we all know now as factthat Saddam Hussein had no weapons of mass destruction.  And as then, there was no connection between the horrific events of 9/11 and Iraq.  Iraq did not attack us, as many are trying to continue to convince the American people that it did.  Iraq didn't attack us five years ago. The bottom line is that Iraq also would not be a war torn country, as it is today, and, again, the world is less safe.   And, if this wasn't enough, over the last several months, the President has been saber-rattling on the issue of Iran.  We must not go down the same path and end up in another unnecessary, dangerous, costly and disastrous preemptive war with Iran. This notion of the axis of evil' and preemptive war is very dangerous. Madame Speaker, the stakes are too high.  We need to stop digging ourselves deeper into this hole. Escalating this war and expanding this war does nothing in terms of our national security.  It puts us more at risk.  Iraq was not a haven for terrorists, as it is now.  Again, Iraq, Saddam Hussein and al Qaeda, there was no connection and we have to dispel that notion so the American people know the truth. So, rather than try to end this war today, we are saying, for today at least, let's stop the escalation and expansion, and we'll be back to talk about how we're going to begin to bring our troops home and bring them home within six months. ###

title: Barbara Lee Delivers Third Annual State of the District Address
text:
02.25.07
			 Barbara Lee Delivers Third Annual State of the District Address 
			(Oakland, CA)  Congresswoman Barbara Lee (D-Oakland) gave her third annual State of the District speech at the Ronald V. Dellums Federal Building in downtown Oakland Monday night, discussing her legislative priorities, telling the stories of inspiring constituents and looking at what the new, Democratic Congress means for progressives.  The following are select excerpts from the speech (as prepared for delivery): Iraq  For those of us who opposed the war from the beginning, the nation now stands with us.   The American people spoke in November and what they gave us was a clear mandate to end this war.    The question that we must now ask is how many brave men and women have to give up their lives so the President can avoid admitting he was wrong?    This is not a time to be timid.  The situation in Iraq is a civil war that we cannot win.  We must end this occupation and bring our troops home now. Iran Let me tell you, we are not going to let President Bush mislead this nation into another disastrous preemptive war. Ending Poverty and Fostering Opportunity We have to reject the Bush administration's cynical budget.   We have to reject the idea that prosperity has to come at someone's expense, that the government's only responsibility is to protect business and the wealthy, and the rest will take care of itself.    I am proud to announce that in the 110th Congress I have co-founded and now co-chair the Congressional Out-of-Poverty Caucus, an organization that is committed to focus Congress' attention on ending poverty in the United States.    I recently became a member of the House Appropriations Committee, which controls the federal purse strings, and I serve on the Subcommittee on Labor, Health and Human Services and Education, and one of the commitments I make to you is that I will use my position to fight poverty where it begins, in a system of unequal public education.  That means fighting to fully fund programs like TRIO and GEAR UP, which ensure that high-risk students succeed in high school and move on to college, as well as fighting not just to fully fund, but to reform No Child Left Behind. HIV/AIDS If we are to succeed in fighting this disease, we have to raise the profile of the issue and we have to come together as a community, which is why I am proud to announce that later this week, musician and international AIDS activist Bono will be joining me in Oakland to learn about HIV/AIDS in the African American community and to participate in a roundtable discussion with faith leaders on how we can best fight the disease. We must also go beyond prevention and treatment to address the factors that make certain communities more vulnerable than others.   That means we need to get real about the role that our prison system is playing in the spread of HIV in the Black community. There is a tendency in this country to think that people who go away to prison or jail are going away forever.  The truth is that 95 percent of those people will be returning to our communities.  People need to understand that when a prisoner is infected, we are all affected. Serving Constituents Adriana Perez Here in the East Bay, we have been working with partners in the community like the East Bay Consortium.  For over four years, we have been organizing Cash for College workshops that are designed to help students and their families by making getting financial aid as easy and accessible as possible.  Last year, a young woman from Oakland named Adriana Perez attended the Cash for College workshop at Laney College with her mother and her sister. With the help of the volunteers at the workshop, Adriana received a Pell Grant, a Cal Grant, an Academic Competitiveness Grant, an Educational Opportunity Program Grant, a Supplemental Educational Opportunity Grant, and Work-study.  By saving money living at home and by finding scholarships to help pay for books, Adriana is able to go to college without taking out loans.  Today, Adriana is a sophomore at San Francisco State University, where she plans on declaring her major in Speech and Communications with the idea of becoming a program director for youth organizations in the Bay Area.  Adriana is here with us tonight.  Adriana, will you stand up and take a bow?  We are so proud of you, and we wish you all the best, because the Bay Area needs your energy and your passion and your leadership.  Raynetta Lewis Just as everybody deserves access to the opportunities afforded by education, people also deserve second chances.  Ten thousand people return from prison or jail every year to live in Alameda County, and let me tell you, we all have a vested interest in making sure that they do so successfully. Formerly incarcerated people face difficulties in finding work, acquiring stable housing, pursuing education and otherwise stabilizing their lives.  These difficulties contribute to recidivism and act as a barrier to the economic and social development of the entire community. That is why we set out to work with our partners in the community, like the East Bay Community Law Center, to help people clean up their criminal records and put their pasts behind them. The result has been a series of Clean Slate Summits, and the establishment of Criminal Record Clinics that together have helped more than 2,500 people clear up their criminal records.  One person who participated in our first record remedy summit at Laney College is with us here tonight. After she got out of prison more than eight years ago, Raynetta Lewis worked to turn her life around, eventually becoming a program coordinator at the Seventh Step Foundation in Hayward, where she draws on her experience to help others turn their lives around.  Still, she had a criminal record going back to the 70's, and she didn't think she could ever put it behind her. After attending our first Clean Slate Summit at Laney College and working with the East Bay Community Law Center, she has just this week received her Certificate of Rehabilitation.  She will now be pursuing a pardon from the Governor.  Raynetta, could you stand up and take a bow! We are so proud of you, and what you have done.  Your experience and your perseverance are an inspiration. For those who say that we can't afford to give people like Raynetta a second chance, I say we can't afford not to.  Sorreya Moody Finally, part of our work is giving young people the opportunity to learn about how our government works - either through internships, fellowships or serving in the Congressional Page Program - so that they have the tools, the understanding and the experience to be our future leaders.  So I would like to congratulate Sorreya Moody from Ashland, who attends San Lorenzo High School and who is in Washington, DC now serving as the Congressional Page from our district from January until June.  Her mom Shaunice is with us tonight.  Please join me in congratulating her! We are expecting great things from Sorreya. What the Democratic Majority Means for Progressives As Democrats, we took control of Congress with a clear sense of our responsibility to make comprehensive reforms and to restore public trust in our government, and as a Senior Democratic Whip, I was proud to work with Speaker Pelosi and our leadership to move the 100 hour agenda.     It was a Herculean effort to achieve so much in so little time, but that was just the beginning.  Our accomplishments in the first 100 hours constitute a courageous step in the right direction, but as progressives we have to go further.   We must keep the pressure on.    A year ago, I came before you and challenged you to bring accountability back to Washington, to bring new leaders w

title: Barbara Lees Website Wins 2006 Bronze Mouse Award  615 House and Senate Web sites Examined in 2006 Gold Mouse Report
text:
02.27.07
			 Barbara Lee's Website Wins 2006 Bronze Mouse Award  615 House and Senate Web sites Examined in 2006 Gold Mouse Report  
			Washington, D.C.  The web site of Congresswoman Barbara Lee (D-Oakland) was honored by the Congressional Management Foundation, a non-profit, non-partisan management consulting and research organization in Washington, D.C., for being one of the best in Congress. The site, http://lee.house.gov, was one of 615 evaluated in The 2006 Gold Mouse Report: Recognizing the Best Web Sites on Capitol Hill, which analyzed congressional Web sites including those of all Senate and House Members, committees (both majority and minority sites), and official leadership sites.  Eighty-five Web sites were cited in 2006, with 18 winning Gold, 27 winning Silver, and 40 winning Bronze Mouse Awards.    We have worked hard to try to use the internet to connect with constituents, so it is really nice to have that work recognized, said Lee. The Gold Mouse Report and Awards are part of the Congressional Management Foundation's broader research project, Connecting to Congress, funded by a grant from the National Science Foundation. For this project CMF partnered with researchers from the John F. Kennedy School of Government at Harvard University, University of California-Riverside and Ohio State University to study how Members of Congress can use the Internet to improve communications with their constituents and to promote greater participation in the legislative process.  One of the key purposes of the awards is to highlight best practices so offices can improve their sites by learning from those doing a good job, said Beverly Bell, executive director of the Congressional Management Foundation. Web sites that garnered an Award in 2006 illustrate the best practices that we hope can serve as examples for others to follow.  Congresswoman Lee's Web site shows that she has identified the needs of her various constituencies and has developed easily accessible content that meets their needs online, said Bell.  Congresswoman Lee is to be congratulated for being among the best of the best Web sites on Capitol Hill, and the Congressional Management Foundation is pleased to present her with the 2006 Bronze Mouse Award. The 2006 report shows that Web sites are an increasingly critical channel through which Members communicate to, and hear from, constituents.  In a time when people can do everything online from renewing their driver licenses to paying their taxes, they understandably expect to find information on the Web about legislation and congressional assistance with federal agencies, as well as the opportunity to communicate their own views, Bell said. Web sites were graded on how well they incorporate five basic building blocks that extensive research has identified as critical for effectiveness:   audience, content, usability, interactivity, and innovation. Using these building blocks, an evaluation framework was developed by CMF and their research partners at Harvard, Ohio State, and the University of California-Riverside that would be fair and objective, while still taking into account important qualitative factors that affect a visitor's experience on a Web site.    CMF will be assessing congressional Web sites again in 2007.  All House and Senate Member, committee and leadership Web sites will be evaluated during the summer of 2007 and another report is expected to be released by the end of the year.  A full copy of the report, the 2006 Gold Mouse Report: Recognizing the Best Web Sites on Capitol Hill, is available on the Congressional Management Foundation's Web site at www.cmfweb.org  ### 
	
	  

	
	
	
	
	
	
	

 
	    	  
		    
		    	Next Article
